toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload (dotnet watch) for their test project without having to set an environment variable every time. For `dotnet test`, hot reload is typically done via `dotnet watch test`. But the user mentions not wanting to set an environment variable - this likely refers to `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART_ON_RUDE_EDIT` or similar.

Actually, thinking about this more carefully - the user is likely referring to `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` or the hot reload feature in `dotnet watch`. But for test projects specifically, the common scenario is using `dotnet watch test` which re-runs tests when files change.


With .NET 9's Microsoft Testing Platform, there's a `--hotreload` flag available for `dotnet test`, though enabling it might require setting an environment variable like `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` - which is probably what they're trying to avoid having to configure manually each time. the project file itself. I could set `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` to include `--hotreload`, or use a `.runsettings` file or `launchSettings.json` to configure it. The most practical approach is probably using `dotnet watch test` with a `launchSettings.json`, or adding an MSBuild property to the project file to make hot reload persistent without needing environment variables each time. I'm reconsidering the approach here. The cleanest solution would be to set the environment variable in `launchSettings.json` or use `dotnet watch test` with a script, but the most practical option for the Microsoft Testing Platform is probably to configure it directly in the project file using an MSBuild property that enables hot reload by default. I'm realizing that `EnvironmentVariables` isn't the right MSBuild property for this. The better approach is to use the testing platform's configuration—either through a `testconfig.json` file or by passing default CLI arguments, so I don't have to manually add `--hotreload` every time I run tests. I'm going in circles here. Let me focus on what actually works: the `--hotreload` flag works with `dotnet test`, and for persisting this as a default, a `.runsettings` file or environment variable in the project file would be the most reliable approach, though I'm not entirely certain about the exact configuration schema for the Testing Platform. With .NET 9's testing platform, I can enable hot reload directly in the project file using a specific property, or leverage `dotnet watch test` which has built-in support. The cleanest approach is probably just using `dotnet test --hotreload` from the command line, though I should verify what the actual flag or configuration option is. I'm realizing the cleanest approach is to set the `TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled` property in the project file to make hot reload the default behavior for `dotnet test`, rather than relying on command-line flags each time. I need to clarify what the user actually wants—whether they're looking to avoid typing `--hotreload` every time, use `dotnet watch test` for automatic hot reload, or set an environment variable. Let me think through what the right property or configuration would be for the project file to enable this by default. I'm going in circles here—let me just try the straightforward approach and check if the .NET 9 SDK supports a testing platform configuration file or an MSBuild property for hot reload, then implement whichever is most reliable. Configuring the .runsettings file... I should just run `dotnet test --help` to see what hot reload options are actually available, then implement the most straightforward approach based on what the tooling supports.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  .NET Test Driver

Usage:
  dotnet test [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.
  -e, --environment <NAME="VALUE">     Sets the value of an environment variable. 
                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process. 
                                       This argument can be specified multiple times to provide multiple variables.
  
                                       Examples:
                                       -e VARIABLE=abc
                                       -e VARIABLE="value with spaces"
                                       -e VARIABLE="value;seperated with;semicolons"
                                       -e VAR1=abc -e VAR2=def -e VAR3=ghi
  --filter <EXPRESSION>                Run tests that match the given expression.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Run tests with priority set to 1: 
                                       --filter "Priority = 1"
                                                                               Run a test with the specified full name: 
                                       --filter "FullyQualifiedName=Namespace.ClassName.MethodName"
                                                                               Run tests that contain the specified 
                                       name: --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Namespace.Class"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-filtering for 
                                       more information on filtering support.
  --test-adapter-path <ADAPTER_PATH>   The path to the custom adapters to use for the test run.
  -l, --logger <LOGGER>                The logger to use for test results.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Log in trx format using a unique file 
                                       name: --logger trx
                                                                               Log in trx format using the specified 
                                       file name: --logger "trx;LogFileName=<TestResults.trx>"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-report for 
                                       more information on logger arguments.
  -o, --output <OUTPUT_DIR>            The output directory to place built artifacts in.
  --artifacts-path <ARTIFACTS_DIR>     The artifacts path. All output from the project, including build, publish, and 
                                       pack output, will go in subfolders under the specified path.
  -d, --diag <LOG_FILE>                Enable verbose logging to the specified file.
  --no-build                           Do not build the project before testing. Implies --no-restore.
  --results-directory <RESULTS_DIR>    The directory where the test results will be placed.
                                       The specified directory will be created if it does not exist.
  --collect <DATA_COLLECTOR_NAME>      The friendly name of the data collector to use for the test run.
                                                                               More info here: 
                                       https://aka.ms/vstest-collect
  --blame                              Runs the tests in blame mode. This option is helpful in isolating problematic 
                                       tests that cause the test host to crash or hang, but it does not create a memory 
                                       dump by default. 
  
                                       When a crash is detected, it creates an sequence file in 
                                       TestResults/guid/guid_Sequence.xml that captures the order of tests that were 
                                       run before the crash.
  
                                       Based on the additional settings, hang dump or crash dump can also be collected.
  
                                       Example: 
                                         Timeout the test run when test takes more than the default timeout of 1 hour,
<exited with exit code 0>

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  
  
  Environment variables:
  
    DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will poll the file system for
    changes. This is required for some file systems, such as network shares,
    Docker mounted volumes, and other virtual file systems.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' on all child processes launched.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_ITERATION
    dotnet-watch sets this variable to '1' and increments by one each time
    a file is changed and the command is restarted.
  
    DOTNET_WATCH_SUPPRESS_EMOJIS
    When set to '1' or 'true', dotnet-watch will not show emojis in the 
    console output.
  
  Remarks:
    The special option '--' is used to delimit the end of the options and
    the beginning of arguments that will be passed to the child dotnet process.
  
    For example: dotnet watch -- --verbose run
  
    Even though '--verbose' is an option dotnet-watch supports, the use of '--'
    indicates that '--verbose' should be treated instead as an argument for
    dotnet-run.
  
  Examples:
    dotnet watch run
    dotnet watch test
  
      

Usage:
  dotnet-watch [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]
<exited with exit code 0>

data.reasoningText:
So `dotnet test` doesn't seem to have a built-in `--hotreload` flag in this version. Let me check what version of .NET this is and what the testing platform options are. Let me also check `dotnet watch` options more carefully.

Actually, the `dotnet watch test` command already provides hot reload / file watching. The user probably wants to use `dotnet watch test` but wants to configure it so hot reload is always enabled.

Let me check if this is about `dotnet watch` or something specific to the testing platform. Looking at the `dotnet watch --help` output, the key env vars are:
- `DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER` - for using polling


- `DOTNET_WATCH` - set by dotnet-watch on child processes

The user mentioned not wanting to set an environment variable every time, which suggests they know about an env var that controls this behavior and want a permanent configuration. Let me look at the `dotnet test` options directly to see if there's a `--hotreload` flag available in the testing platform, or check if there's a way to configure this in a project file or settings.

I should build and run the tests to see what options the test executable actually supports.

arguments.description: Check .NET version and remaining test help

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-af9b07d919ff4833b37bcf6703297810 && dotnet build -q 2>&1 && dotnet exec bin/Debug/net9.0/TestProject.dll --help 2>&1 | grep -i "hot" || echo "No hot reload in test runner help"
arguments.description: Build and check test runner for hot reload

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-af9b07d919ff4833b37bcf6703297810 && dotnet --version 2>&1 && dotnet test --help 2>&1 | tail -30", "description": "Check .NET version and remaining test help", "initial_wait": 15}

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-af9b07d919ff4833b37bcf6703297810 && dotnet build -q 2>&1 && dotnet exec bin/Debug/net9.0/TestProject.dll --help 2>&1 | grep -i \"hot\" || echo \"No hot reload in test runner help\"", "description": "Build and check test runner for hot reload", "initial_wait": 30}

toolArgs.description: Check .NET version and remaining test help

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
9.0.314
                                       mini, and none. When 'none' is used then test host is terminated on timeout, but 
                                       no dump is collected. Implies --blame-hang.
  --blame-hang-timeout <TIMESPAN>      Per-test timeout, after which hang dump is triggered and the testhost process is 
                                       terminated. Default is 1h.
                                       The timeout value is specified in the following format: 1.5h / 90m / 5400s / 
                                       5400000ms. When no unit is used (e.g. 5400000), the value is assumed to be in 
                                       milliseconds.
                                       When used together with data driven tests, the timeout behavior depends on the 
                                       test adapter used. For xUnit, NUnit and MSTest 2.2.4+ the timeout is renewed 
                                       after every test case,
                                       For MSTest before 2.2.4, the timeout is used for all testcases.
  --nologo                             Run test(s), without displaying Microsoft Testplatform banner
  -c, --configuration <CONFIGURATION>  The configuration to use for running tests. The default for most projects is 
                                       'Debug'.
  -f, --framework <FRAMEWORK>          The target framework to run tests for. The target framework must also be 
                                       specified in the project file.
  -r, --runtime <RUNTIME_IDENTIFIER>   The target runtime to test for.
  --no-restore                         Do not restore the project before building.
  --interactive                        Allows the command to stop and wait for user input or action (for example to 
                                       complete authentication).
  -v, --verbosity <LEVEL>              Set the MSBuild verbosity level. Allowed values are q[uiet], m[inimal], 
                                       n[ormal], d[etailed], and diag[nostic].
  -a, --arch <ARCH>                    The target architecture.
  --os <OS>                            The target operating system.
  --disable-build-servers              Force the command to ignore any persistent build servers.
  -?, -h, --help                       Show command line help.

Additional Arguments:
  Arguments passed to the application that is being run.
<exited with exit code 0>
